annotations.label: Tree (lead)

anchor.snippet: a tree is an undirected graph in which every pair of distinct vertices is connected by exactly one path

annotations.note: `isTree_iff_existsUnique_path` states a graph is a tree iff the vertex type is nonempty and every pair of vertices is joined by a unique path.

annotations.label: Forest (lead)

anchor.snippet: A forest is an undirected graph in which any two vertices are connected by at most one path

annotations.note: `IsAcyclic` is defined as having no cycles, and `isAcyclic_iff_path_unique` characterizes a forest as having at most one path between any two vertices.

annotations.label: Polytree / directed tree

anchor.snippet: A directed tree,

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the rooted directed-tree (arborescence) variant via `Quiver.Arborescence`, but not the general polytree (DAG whose underlying graph is a tree).

annotations.label: Polyforest

anchor.snippet: A polyforest (or directed forest or oriented forest) is a directed acyclic graph whose underlying undirected graph is a forest.

annotations.note: No definition of a polyforest (DAG whose underlying graph is a forest) was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rooted tree, arborescence, anti-arborescence

anchor.snippet: such data structures are generally rooted trees

annotations.note: `Quiver.Arborescence` formalizes an arborescence (unique path from root to every vertex), but anti-arborescences and the general undirected-rooted-tree notion are not formalized.

annotations.label: Rooted forest, branching, anti-branching

anchor.snippet: A rooted forest is a disjoint union of rooted trees.

annotations.note: No notion of rooted forest, branching, or anti-branching was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tree (equivalent conditions)

anchor.snippet: A tree is an undirected graph G that satisfies any of the following equivalent conditions

annotations.note: Several equivalences are proved: `isTree_iff_existsUnique_path`, `isTree_iff_minimal_connected`, `maximal_isAcyclic_iff_isTree`, and `isTree_iff_maximal_isAcyclic`.

annotations.label: Finite tree equivalent conditions

anchor.snippet: If G has finitely many vertices, say n of them, then the above statements are also equivalent to any of the following conditions

annotations.note: `isTree_iff_connected_and_card` proves that, over a finite vertex type, a graph is a tree iff it is connected and has exactly `n - 1` edges.

annotations.label: Order-zero graph as forest

anchor.snippet: the order-zero graph (graph with no vertices) is generally not considered to be a tree

annotations.note: `isAcyclic_bot` shows the empty graph is acyclic, and `IsTree` requires `Connected` (hence Nonempty V), so the convention is implicitly captured but not articulated as such.

annotations.label: Internal, external, branch vertex

anchor.snippet: An internal vertex (or inner vertex) is a vertex of degree at least 2.

annotations.note: Mathlib has `SimpleGraph.degree` but no named predicates for internal/external/branch vertices.

annotations.label: Irreducible tree

anchor.snippet: An irreducible tree (or series-reduced tree) is a tree in which there is no vertex of degree 2

annotations.note: No notion of irreducible/series-reduced tree was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Forest

anchor.snippet: A forest is an undirected acyclic graph or equivalently a disjoint union of trees.

annotations.note: `IsAcyclic` is defined directly as having no cyclic walk; `IsAcyclic.isTree_connectedComponent` shows the disjoint-union-of-trees view.

annotations.label: Counting trees in a forest

anchor.snippet: we can easily count the number of trees that are within a forest by subtracting the difference between total vertices and total edges

annotations.note: Mathlib has `IsTree.card_edgeFinset` (n = m+1 for a tree) but no theorem expressing #components = #vertices − #edges for a forest.

annotations.label: Polytree

anchor.snippet: is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) whose underlying undirected graph is a tree

annotations.note: Only the rooted/arborescence special case appears in Mathlib; the general polytree (DAG whose underlying graph is a tree) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Polyforest

anchor.snippet: A polyforest (or directed forest or oriented forest) is a directed acyclic graph whose underlying undirected graph is a forest.

annotations.note: No definition of polyforest was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rooted tree, arborescence, in-tree

anchor.snippet: A rooted tree is a tree in which one vertex has been designated the root.

annotations.note: `Quiver.Arborescence` covers out-arborescences from a root; in-trees and a generic undirected-graph + designated-root rooted tree are not formalized.

annotations.label: Tree-order

anchor.snippet: The tree-order is the partial ordering on the vertices of a tree

annotations.note: `Mathlib.Order.SuccPred.Tree` models a rooted tree as a `PartialOrder` with `PredOrder`+`IsPredArchimedean`+`OrderBot`, encoding the tree-order, but it is not connected to `SimpleGraph` trees.

annotations.label: Normal tree

anchor.snippet: is a normal tree if the ends of every

annotations.note: No notion of normal tree was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Free tree

anchor.snippet: a tree without any designated root is called a free tree

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsTree` is a connected acyclic graph with no designated root, matching the notion of a free tree.

annotations.label: Labeled tree

anchor.snippet: A labeled tree is a tree in which each vertex is given a unique label.

annotations.note: No labeled-tree concept beyond the implicit vertex-type indexing was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursive tree

anchor.snippet: A recursive tree is a labeled rooted tree where the vertex labels respect the tree order

annotations.note: No recursive-tree definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parent, child, ascendant, descendant, sibling, leaf

anchor.snippet: In a rooted tree, the parent of a vertex

annotations.note: `BinaryTree` (and `Order.SuccPred.Tree` via `Order.pred`) encode parent/child relations in their respective settings, but Mathlib has no graph-theoretic parent/child/leaf API for `SimpleGraph` rooted trees.

annotations.label: Height and depth

anchor.snippet: The height of a vertex in a rooted tree is the length of the longest downward path to a leaf from that vertex.

annotations.note: `BinaryTree.height` defines tree height for the binary-tree datatype only; no graph-theoretic height/depth on `SimpleGraph` rooted trees was found.

annotations.label: k-ary tree, binary tree, ternary tree

anchor.snippet: is a rooted tree in which each vertex has at most

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes `BinaryTree` as a recursive datatype, but no general k-ary or ternary tree predicate over graphs is provided.

annotations.label: Ordered (plane) tree

anchor.snippet: An ordered tree (alternatively, plane tree or positional tree

annotations.note: No ordered/plane-tree definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Every tree is bipartite

anchor.snippet: Every tree is a bipartite graph

annotations.note: `IsTree.isBipartite` (via the explicit two-coloring `IsTree.coloringTwo`) proves every tree is bipartite.

annotations.label: Countable trees are planar

anchor.snippet: Every tree with only countably many vertices is a planar graph

annotations.note: Planarity of graphs is not formalized in Mathlib (only mentioned in a docstring).

annotations.label: Spanning tree existence

anchor.snippet: Every connected graph G admits a spanning tree

annotations.note: `Connected.exists_isTree_le` proves every connected graph has a spanning subgraph that is a tree.

annotations.label: Trémaux tree existence

anchor.snippet: every connected graph with only countably many vertices has a Trémaux tree

annotations.note: No Trémaux/DFS tree existence result was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Leaves in finite trees

anchor.snippet: Every finite tree with n vertices, with n > 1 , has at least two terminal vertices (leaves).

annotations.note: `IsTree.exists_vert_degree_one_of_nontrivial` gives the existence of one degree-1 vertex (one leaf), but the stronger "at least two leaves" statement was not found.

annotations.label: Tree as median graph

anchor.snippet: For any three vertices in a tree, the three paths between them have exactly one vertex in common.

annotations.note: No median-graph or Steiner-point property of trees was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Center and centroid of a tree

anchor.snippet: Every tree has a center consisting of one vertex or two adjacent vertices.

annotations.note: No notion of graph center/centroid for trees was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Maximal cliques of a tree

anchor.snippet: The maximal cliques of a tree are precisely its edges

annotations.note: Although Mathlib has `SimpleGraph.IsClique`, the specific characterization of maximal cliques of a tree was not found.

annotations.label: Cayley's formula

anchor.snippet: Cayley's formula states that there are

annotations.note: Cayley's formula counting labeled trees by n^{n-2} was not found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix tree theorem

anchor.snippet: A more general problem is to count spanning trees in an undirected graph , which is addressed by the matrix tree theorem .

annotations.note: `LapMatrix` defines the graph Laplacian and proves rank-related results, but Kirchhoff's matrix-tree theorem (cofactor counts spanning trees) was not found.

annotations.label: Otter's asymptotic estimate (unlabeled trees)

anchor.snippet: Otter (1948) proved the asymptotic estimate

annotations.note: No asymptotic count of unlabeled trees (Otter's theorem) was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Otter's asymptotic estimate (rooted trees)

anchor.snippet: This is a consequence of his asymptotic estimate for the number

annotations.note: No asymptotic count of rooted unlabeled trees was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Path graph

anchor.snippet: A path graph (or linear graph ) consists of

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.pathGraph n` is the path graph on `Fin n`, with adjacency and connectedness lemmas.

annotations.label: Starlike tree

anchor.snippet: A starlike tree consists of a central vertex called root and several path graphs attached to it.

annotations.note: No starlike-tree definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Star tree

anchor.snippet: A star tree is a tree which consists of a single internal vertex

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.starGraph r` is the star graph centered at `r`, and `isTree_starGraph` proves it is a tree.

annotations.label: Caterpillar tree

anchor.snippet: A caterpillar tree is a tree in which all vertices are within distance 1 of a central path subgraph.

annotations.note: No caterpillar-tree definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lobster tree

anchor.snippet: A lobster tree is a tree in which all vertices are within distance 2 of a central path subgraph.

annotations.note: No lobster-tree definition was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular tree / Bethe lattice

anchor.snippet: A regular tree of degree

annotations.note: No regular-tree or Bethe-lattice definition was found in Mathlib.
